title: Do You Think Shinji deserves all the fan hate
display_title: Do You Think Shinji deserves all the fan hate
wikitext:
[]: Do You Think Shinji deserves all the fan hate? Post by Negi Springfield » Tue Sep 21, 2010 9:41 pm I gave this some thought, and I can't say that he does. I don't know why I'm more sympathetic to the characters in fiction who have limitations or problems. Maybe I can relate, since I'm not the popular guy. I do feel like Shinji at times and it's hard at times. I think that's why I like Neon Genesis Evangelion, because I find Misato hot and Shinji familiar. He's the kind of kid you wish you could talk to, just to perhaps help and give support. Maybe that's just me, but does anyone else wish they could help Shinji? Besides that, does anyone else relate to NGE in some way? Please tell...

[]: Post by IrkenEvangelion » Tue Sep 21, 2010 9:44 pm Shinji's a dick!!.... haha no seriously, he has his issues that he deals with in... unique ways, and he does have it pretty hard, you know having to save the world and whatnot. But he can be a pansie a lot of the time.

[]: Post by MugwumpHasNoLiver » Tue Sep 21, 2010 10:11 pm No way. I was just as bad at fourteen.

[]: Post by Lucretius » Tue Sep 21, 2010 10:16 pm I've taken advantage of comatose teenage girls too, so I can really identify with Shinji.

[]: Post by Sachi » Tue Sep 21, 2010 10:21 pm IrkenEvangelion wrote: Shinji's a dick!!....

[]: Post by Cody MacArthur Fett » Tue Sep 21, 2010 10:27 pm Shinji really is a terrible person who fails to rise to the challenge and become a hero, but the thing is that taking in account his psych profile and the circumstances he was placed in he really didn't have a chance. I mean, my goodness man, the events of NGE take place over a three and a half month timespan at the most , when was the last time you had a 180 degree shift in personality in less then four months? Shinji was a character who had serious mental issues requiring months of therapy and years of proper socialization to get over, and he simply did not get the chance to have that. Heck, none of the characters got the chance to get over themselves and work things out, they were just thrown into a war with minimal psych support and expected to do their jobs under incredibly stressful conditions with a smile and a wave, Shinji just gets most of the flak because he's the protagonist and we see the most of him.

[]: Post by esselfortium » Tue Sep 21, 2010 10:30 pm Cody MacArthur Fett wrote: I mean, my goodness man, the events of NGE take place over a three and a half month timespan at the most

[]: Post by Lucretius » Tue Sep 21, 2010 10:33 pm In all seriousness, I think most people hate Shinji because he's a pretty normal guy in a genre usually reserved for dick waving macho men. Robots aside, you could imagine most of his lines being said by Michael Cera. He's not even particularly sick, really, just a nebbishy everyman placed in extreme situations.

[]: Post by IrkenEvangelion » Tue Sep 21, 2010 10:36 pm Lucretius wrote: Michael Cera.

[]: Post by Cody MacArthur Fett » Tue Sep 21, 2010 10:39 pm esselfortium wrote: I wanted to a bunch of parts of this post, but I'm just going to this part in particular. Here you go:

[]: Post by esselfortium » Tue Sep 21, 2010 10:48 pm There are a lot of people who disagree with me on this, but I'm of the opinion that the two or three dates shown onscreen were largely bullshitted, and that NGE's timeline wasn't really considered of great importance during the show's development. If I take your timeline at face value, Shinji likely spent more days as LCL inside Eva-01 than he actually spent being friends with Toji before having to maim him. That seems off. In any case, Shinji's progression from zero to hero back to zero again becomes less and less believable to me the fewer months one tries to squeeze it into. Even moreso when considering that he's supposed to grow close enough to the people around him for their collective unraveling to have such a deep effect on him (this goes for all the characters, really).

[]: Post by Lucretius » Tue Sep 21, 2010 11:26 pm Cody MacArthur Fett wrote: there's no way Shinji could have developed as a character enough to ultimately save the world in the time alloted.

[]: Post by Azathoth » Wed Sep 22, 2010 12:04 am Lucretius wrote: Shinji saves the world in just about every episode not numbered 25' or 26'.

[]: Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Sep 22, 2010 12:26 am I don't know about deserve, but I think it's good that it does because having a provocative character that provokes such strong reactions as he does is usually a positive sign for a work of art. I'm, of course, a defender of Shinji because I empathized with him so strongly, and I think what makes him so powerful is that a lot of people do, and the people that don't just have zero insight into why he (or anyone) would be that way (often not realizing that they, themselves, are like that).

[]: Post by Merridian » Wed Sep 22, 2010 1:12 am I enjoy my fiction because I like my heroes. I enjoy indulging in entertainment in which the protagonists are admirable, ambitious, macho, moral, and confident. When I escape into my fictional worlds that I either take in through others’ visions or by sculpting my own, I do not search for reality or reflections of things I encounter every day. I look for strength, and passion, and doing what is right. Shinji does admirable things. He also complains a lot. Sometimes he complains while he’s doing the admirable things. Every once in awhile he does something really stupid and deserves to be marked with scorn. He is dynamic and relatable, but at the same time, he exists in a world that throws realism in the audience’s face countless times. Character dynamics that might as well mimic things I myself have experienced are pasted upon an environment with unfathomable monsters, looming genocidal fears, and demon-shaped giant robots that exist to redeem mankind. To say that he is a reflection of what many of us see in ourselves ignores the fact that he pilots a giant robot and is coerced into saving the world time and time again. To say that he is unrealistic because he pilots a giant robot ignores the fact that he many many fans of the show identify with his struggles and his problems. I enjoy fiction for characters like Captain Harlock and Batman, King Arthur, Sam Spade and Clem Snide. I don’t want realism. However, Shinji exists in that blurry area between utterly unreal and depressingly relatable, juxtaposing two extremes as naturally as if they weren’t even there. In fiction, he accomplishes more than I could ever hope for in my whole life, yet at the same time, makes mistakes and remains short-sighted enough to remind me that he is, after all, a character intended to be fourteen years old. Many people who hate Shinji’s character seem to forget this. At the times in which Shinji is most heroic, he is a character deserving our admiration. At the times in which he is at his lowest, he is similarly deserving of our scorn. No amount of sympathy or understanding can condone certain immoral choices, and no amount of twisted logic can ignore the times in which his positive traits return him to the realm of the fictional hero. More importantly, he is a character that left an impression, and that was good enough for me.

[]: Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Sep 22, 2010 1:32 am Merridian wrote: To say that he is a reflection of what many of us see in ourselves ignores the fact that he pilots a giant robot and is coerced into saving the world time and time again... No amount of sympathy or understanding can condone certain immoral choices...

[]: Post by Merridian » Wed Sep 22, 2010 1:54 am Eva Yojimbo wrote: I don't think that just because Shinji is piloting a bio-mecha to save the world means he can't be a reflection of us. In seeking the visual/narrative allegorical equivalent of dealing with reality while in a position of depression I think it's perfectly acceptable to see Shinji's plight as analogous to our own (assuming we've been in a similar position). The reality of such depression, if observed neutrally from a 3rd party, isn't nearly so impressive. It's the internal battle that makes us feel like we're piloting our own mecha to save the world and, hell, just survive. I think it's precisely because Shinji is going through something that objectively traumatic that he's so relatable.

[]: Post by InstrumentalityOne » Wed Sep 22, 2010 2:23 am He´s a sympathetic character because the fan-base is/was like Shinji more or less.

[]: Post by Mr. Tines » Wed Sep 22, 2010 3:04 am http://forum.evageeks.org/viewtopic.php?t=6154 Personally I think much of the scorn (for series!Shinji) has more to do with the closing remarks of this copypasta Hi, my name is Shinji Ikari. I am a cipher, a symbol, a representation of human futility and impotency--creative impotency, I mean. Thanks to the narrow-minded dedication of the director and his writing staff, I am the locus of a squandered plot. I anthropomorphize failed character development. When you loathe me, it's supposed to be art. But let's be honest, that's bullshit spread over a sewage sandwich. From a characterological perspective, the climax fails spectacularly. The primary conflicts are completely abandoned in exchange for a philosophically shallow, symbolically incoherent ending. Most of the other protagonists are killed or driven nuts, because death is easier to write than emotional maturation. And I think our budget was running out. Did you know that a hentai circle produced a doujin series with a better ending? Yeah. I'm sorry. At least you can pretend that I scored the emotionally vulnerable German chick. I mean, shooting fish in a barrel, and somehow I missed...

[]: Post by DevRei17 » Wed Sep 22, 2010 3:27 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: I don't know about deserve, but I think it's good that it does because having a provocative character that provokes such strong reactions as he does is usually a positive sign for a work of art. I'm, of course, a defender of Shinji because I empathized with him so strongly, and I think what makes him so powerful is that a lot of people do, and the people that don't just have zero insight into why he (or anyone) would be that way (often not realizing that they, themselves, are like that).

I do feel like Shinji at times and it's hard at times. I think that's why I like Neon Genesis Evangelion, because I find Misato hot and Shinji familiar. He&#39

Article: Do You Think Shinji deserves all the fan hate
 relatable, but at the same time, he exists in a world that throws realism in the audience’s face countless times. Character dynamics that might as well mimic things I myself have experienced are pasted upon an environment with unfathomable monsters, looming genocidal fears, and demon-shaped giant robots that exist to redeem mankind. To say that he is a reflection of what many of us see in ourselves ignores the fact that he pilots a giant robot and is coerced into saving the world time and time again. To say that he is unrealistic because he pilots a giant robot ignores the fact that he many many fans of the show identify with his struggles and his problems. I enjoy fiction for characters like Captain Harlock and Batman, King Arthur, Sam Spade and Clem Snide. I don’t want realism. However, Shinji exists in that blurry area between utterly unreal and depressingly relatable, juxtaposing two extremes as naturally as if they weren’t even there. In fiction, he accomplishes more than I could ever hope for in my whole life, yet at the same time, makes mistakes and remains short-sighted enough to remind me that he is, after all, a character intended to be fourteen years old. Many people who hate Shinji’s character seem to forget this. At the times in which Shinji is most heroic, he is a character deserving our admiration. At the times in which he is at his lowest, he is similarly deserving of our scorn. No amount of sympathy or understanding can condone certain immoral choices, and no amount of twisted logic can ignore the times in which his positive traits return him to the realm of the fictional hero. More importantly, he is a character that left an impression, and that was good enough for me.

Article: Do You Think Shinji deserves all the fan hate
 think that just because Shinji is piloting a bio-mecha to save the world means he can't be a reflection of us. In seeking the visual/narrative allegorical equivalent of dealing with reality while in a position of depression I think it's perfectly acceptable to see Shinji's plight as analogous to our own (assuming we've been in a similar position). The reality of such depression, if observed neutrally from a 3rd party, isn't nearly so impressive. It's the internal battle that makes us feel like we're piloting our own mecha to save the world and, hell, just survive. I think it's precisely because Shinji is going through something that objectively traumatic that he's so relatable.
